speaker: Bob Kendrick
text: Let me welcome the Negro League Baseball Museum President Bob Kendrick's to the Madison Show here in the Eagles Nest and Bob. Thank you so much. I got to tell you though, I do want to talk about how the museum is coming along. But this news we re-received the other day about a four million dollar renovation project was delayed by vandals back in late June. What happened? How did the vandalism take place? Well, what we know is that someone broke into the developing Buckel Neal education and research center just about three weeks ago. And clearly went in with the attempt of maliciously damaging the building because what they did was they went up to a second floor area that was undeveloped. And we had ran a water line up as we were varying to try and do some finishing to that space. And they cut the main line water pipe causing some severe water damage flooding to the building. And so, yeah, I don't think it was a random act where someone was trying to steal something. I think someone went into the building with a very much intent of damaging the building. And so it was devastating for us. And I think some of this thing is starting to wear off now. I think, you know, as human beings, what really strikes you is the fact that someone would maliciously do something like, I think that hurt just as much because had it been a water pipe that just broke on its own accidents happened, we would have suffered the same level of damage. But I wouldn't have felt so disheartened as you do when you know that someone deliberately and intently did something to destroy what the community was so excited about. And so, yeah, that knocked a little bit of the winds out of ourselves, but we're resilient bunching and we're kind of dusting ourselves off. Getting back in the baddest fox so to say. Yeah, yeah. Yeah, yeah, you know, you've got to keep on doing what you need to do. And we won't let the haters get the last word here. Now, it makes us even more resolved to get books, education and research center. And we're talking about Jordan Buck O'Neill, who was a trailblazer in the Negro League community. And you got to know him really well, didn't you? Oh, I knew Buck long time. He was one of my best friends. And let me let me tell you something, Jill, by saying you know, Buck and I probably have a, oh god, a 50 year age difference. But he was one of my very best friends. And I feel so tremendously blessed to have happy opportunity to spend so many years working alongside right beside traveling with the legendary Buck O'Neill, who was one of the most amazing human beings. I think they ever walked the face of the desert. He was a great baseball player. Well, that's true too. He's a big baseball player. But he was an even greater human being. And you know, he was so excited before he passed away in06 to know that we were going to take the old form of Pavilion YMCA, which is the birthplace of the Negro League. That's where the Negro League performed in Kansas City in 1910. Now, we're talking Kansas City of Missouri. Okay, just want, because I went to school in Washington, you and Missouri. I always want to make sure that we make that distinction. Right. This is Kansas City, Missouri. Right. The leagues were formed in Kansas City, Missouri in 1920 in that very building, the Paseo White. Wow. And so Buck was ecstatic to know that we had taken ownership of that old building that had been left abandoned. It was left abandoned. It was, you know, facing the erosions of time. The Negro League Museum assumed ownership and started to redevelop it to create this education and research center in Buck's memory. And education was always at the forefront of this man's existence, even though he was denied an opportunity to attend his native territory of the high school because of the color of his skin. But he never, you know, it never killed his understanding of the value of an education that he pitched at at every corner that he turned so that young people would understand and hopefully embrace what education means. And so this research sort of meant a lot to him. And so we've been diligently working on it as you touched on four plus million dollar redevelopment, the project of that old blighted building. And so we had made some great strides and certainly this vandalism set us back a little bit because you know you hate to have to go back and redo what was already written. Oh, absolutely. Now the question, and I were talking to Bob Kendrick, who's president of the Negro League Baseball's Museum. And so now let me ask you, will insurance cover the damage? You know, we are still waiting to see, because we've had an umbrella policy on the building, but there was no content in the building and really thankfully, no memorabilia. Oh, that was going to be my next question. Yeah. Oh, okay. So content in the building is just structural. And so we're right now having ongoing conversations with our insurance company. And you know, we're hopeful that they will do the I.A.K. mess. I didn't understand the real power, the damaging power of water. But I understand it now. Yeah. It gives you a whole new meaning when you hear about a flood, doesn't it? You know, and I've been to a few sites where there have been floods. And people don't realize you. It's just sometimes months and years of planning, because you also have to be concerned about mode. Mode and mildew. Mildew and the whole nine yards. Now, finally, do the police obviously investigating this? And have they indicated if there are any leads or is it just too early to tell? But there is an ongoing investigation. Yeah, there is an ongoing investigation. And so I know that the Kansas City Missouri Police Department and the detectives there, they really want to try and apprehend whoever may have done this. I think the community also wants to see this suspect, whomever may have done this also brought to laws. Well, because I can tell you, the community invested a lot of sweat equity in this building. And the redevelopment of the building. When we started the demolition process in this building, there were people from the community who come in with hard hats and gloves and their boots and they were sweeping debris and they were moving debris and doing whatever they could because they were so excited about this. But not only are we at the Negro Nation's Amalow this hearted by what took place, I think the entire community is as well. Sure. But you know, there was something that Bucklenell always said and he said that people will do bad things and good people will fix them. And after this incident occurred, so many people, not only in Kansas City, but from around the country started immediately, you know, pledging their support to the Negro Nation's Amalow to help with this cleanup effort demolition and the subsequent cleanup effort. And that lifted all of our spirits. And they started unsolicitly sending in donations to support this. And so, you know, every time that you're ready to give up all humanity, something happens and you realize that there is more good people than there are bad people always as them and always will be something to try. We just need to be reminded of that. Well, I'm going to tell you Bob what I'm going to do. I have, I don't know if you're aware of this, but I have a swear jar. Now, I'll tell you what I swear to you. On serious, on serious XM, I am allowed to swear every now and then. And Bob, given all the stuff that's been going on lately, I've had plenty of opera as I've always say, sometimes you got to be profane to be profound. So I have accumulated, as a matter of fact, guys, while the interview is going on, can you look and see how much money we got in a swear jar? Because I'm telling you what, usually I give the money to Lonnie Bunch over at the American Museum. My, you know, first time I did it, I gave him a nice hefty check. And he said, just keep on cursing. Just keep on swearing. And I tell you what I'm going to do. That my staff is counting the money right now. I'm going to take whatever's in that swear jar and they're counting fast, man. I'm going to take whatever's in that swear jar and donate to the Negro League Baseball Museum for whatever y'all needed for, the cleanup or whatever. And that's going to be my donation. Lonnie won't mind. He won't mind. Let me, but now this, I don't know if this is a couple of weeks or a month, the worth is. It's a 207. 207 dollars. You should, we should have turned on the mics a year. It's 5 and 15. 10, 23. Wait, and how much and how much today, Darryl? Three. So let's round it up to $210. So I tell you what I'm going to do, Bob. I'm going to send you this $210 from the Madison Swear jar, okay? And let's do this, guys. Let's put the information on our Facebook. So anybody listening that wants to make a contribution to help with the cleanup and anything else that you need it for, they'll have that on our, we'll put that on our Facebook. Because the attitude is what I appreciate from the people in the community. And most important of I can hear it in your voice. I mean, this could knock the wind out of a lot of people. $4 million renovation is nothing to sneeze at. And for like you said, for somebody to deliberately go into that building, climb up on the second floor, and then knowingly cut the, you know, main water pipe to just, you know, damage this. And think that they're going to sit us back over a little water. I'm not going to let, I'm not going to let it happen. And I can tell in the competitive spirit in which you're talking to us, you're not going to let it happen. So that $210 is coming your way. And... Amen. Thanks, thanks, thanks, thanks, we're coming. And I, and I, because I had, I had it 300 today. This was an interesting day. It could have been a lot more, but I, I restrained myself. I know you, wait a minute, nobody has ever gotten donate. And I'm going to tell you, I've been blessed by even the Episcopal Bishop who said, oh, well, just keep swearing, brother Madison. Just keep swearing, just keep all swearing. Somebody's got to. So good luck to you, really good luck to you. And, and let us know how it turns out. We'll stay in, in, in touch. And let us know how it develops in the whole nine yards. And we'll look forward to staying in continued contact with you. Bob Kendrick, President of the Negro League Baseball Museum. Thank you so much. And, and I bet you you're going to hear from a lot of people. And I appreciate it. You being with us. Well, man, I, I, I, I, I, I thank you enough. Thank you for the opportunity to share this. And, and we appreciate everyone who's assisting us in this. Yeah, the God bless everyone. All right, then you have a great day. Okay. All right. So that's $210 worth of person on his way. Right. On the way, on the way.

note: Discusses Negro Leagues history and vandalism at Buck O'Neil Research Center.
